summary: The Gemara maps the Temple courtyard: six chambers (three north, three south) and seven gates, then resolves where the KG's two chambers were located. The KG's immersion bath (ten immersions on Yom Kippur) was adjacent to the Parhedrin chamber; since the immersion facility was near the Water Gate in the south, the Parhedrin chamber is confirmed in the south. The Gemara then traces the KG's daily routine during the seven days: rise, relieve himself, south to immerse, north to Beit Avtinas for incense practice, full day of service, evening purification sprinkling, south again for immersion, rest in Parhedrin. The page ends with the agency question: are kohanim acting as God's agents or Israel's agents when offering sacrifices?

display.title: Locating the Parhedrin chamber by adjacency, not by direct statement
display.whats: The Gemara maps the Temple's inner courtyard (Azara): six chambers (three along the north wall, three along the south wall) and seven gates. It then locates where the KG's two chambers were within this layout. The Parhedrin chamber (where the KG sleeps during his seven-day separation) is identified, and the question of whether it was on the north or south side of the Azara begins.
display.hint: The Temple's courtyard had a specific layout with named chambers and gates. The KG's location during his seven days matters because his immersions (10 total on Yom Kippur) need to be near the gate where the water is brought in. North or south placement affects the logistics of his preparation.

learning.learnerQuestion: Was the Parhedrin chamber, where the KG sleeps during his seven-day separation, on the north or south side of the Azara?
learning.coreTension: The Mishna never states the Parhedrin chamber's side directly, so the Gemara has to derive its location from an indirect fact: where the KG's immersion bath stood.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara reasons from adjacency: the Mishna in Tamid places the KG's immersion bath next to his Parhedrin chamber, and the Water Gate, where the immersion bath sat, is independently known to be in the south. Since the bath is in the south and the chamber is adjacent to the bath, the chamber must also be in the south.
learning.resolution: The Parhedrin chamber is located on the south side of the Azara, established by inference from its adjacency to the southern immersion bath rather than by a direct textual statement.

takeaway.text: An unstated fact can be derived by chaining two known facts through a shared point of contact: if A is adjacent to B, and B's location is known, A's location follows even without a direct source stating it.

learning.ahaMoment: The Temple was a functioning institutional complex with specialized chambers. The KG's spatial location during preparation is not random; it determines his proximity to key service locations, and that proximity is itself the evidence the Gemara reasons from.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners often look for a single verse or mishna that states the Parhedrin chamber's side outright and get stuck when they cannot find one, missing that the Gemara establishes the location entirely through an adjacency inference from the immersion bath.
learning.memoryAnchor: Azara: six chambers (3N + 3S), 7 gates. Bath is south (Water Gate). Parhedrin is next to the bath, so Parhedrin is south too.

argumentFlow.label: Six Azara chambers: three north, three south
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The inner courtyard has three chambers along the north wall: Chamber of the Exile (Lishkat HaGolah), Chamber of the Hewn Stone (Lishkat HaGazit), and a third. Three along the south wall: Chamber of Parhedrin (KG's separation chamber), Chamber of Parvah, and Chamber of Avtinas.

argumentFlow.label: Seven gates of the Azara
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Azara has seven gates: the Great Gate, the Fuel Gate (Sha'ar HaNitzotz), the Gate of the Firstborn (Sha'ar HaBchorot), the Water Gate (Sha'ar HaMayim), the Gate of the Singing (Sha'ar HaShir), the Gate of the Spark (Sha'ar HaNitzotz), and the Eastern Gate (Sha'ar HaMizrach).

argumentFlow.label: The Water Gate: where Sukkot water is brought
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Water Gate is the gate through which the water for the Sukkot water-drawing (nisuch ha-mayim) was brought. It was located on the south side.

argumentFlow.label: Ten immersions: KG immerses on Yom Kippur
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The KG immerses ten times on Yom Kippur, five times in the sanctified immersion pool (on the roof of the Parvah chamber) and the rest in a regular mikveh. Each immersion corresponds to a service change requiring clean garments.

argumentFlow.label: First immersion: not counted with the Yom Kippur immersions
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The first immersion (the one every person must do before entering the Azara) is not counted among the KG's ten Yom Kippur immersions. All the Yom Kippur immersions must occur in a sanctified place.

argumentFlow.label: Where was the Parhedrin chamber: north or south?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Based on the Mishna in Tamid, the KG's immersion bath was adjacent to his chamber (Parhedrin). Since the immersion bath was in the south (near the Water Gate), the Parhedrin chamber should also be in the south.

sourceRefs.note: South placement of Parhedrin from immersion proximity

argumentFlow.label: The KG's daily routine during the seven days
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Each morning during the seven days, the KG rises, goes to relieve himself, immerses (in the south), goes north to his second chamber to practice the incense-scooping, returns to the Temple for the full-day service, then at dusk goes south again for the purification sprinkling.

concepts.def: The Chamber of Parhedrin - the KG's personal chamber during his seven-day separation before Yom Kippur. He slept there and prepared. The name 'parhedrin' may relate to the word for 'chambers' or 'governors.'
The Parvah Chamber - a chamber on the south side of the Azara with a sanctified immersion pool on its roof. The KG immerses in this pool for his five sanctified immersions on Yom Kippur.

requiresUnderstanding: The Temple's three-court structure (outer, women's, inner/Azara)
The KG's ten immersions on Yom Kippur

misconceptions.misconception: The KG immerses ten times on Yom Kippur because there are ten distinct services requiring it.
misconceptions.correction: There are only five garment changes (golden and white alternating); the ten count reflects the immersions tied to those five changes, not ten separate services.

visualizableElements.desc: Azara floor plan showing six named chambers (3N + 3S), seven gates with names, KG's Parhedrin chamber in south, Parvah chamber roof pool
KG's daily seven-day routine: wake up, relieve self, south for immersion, north to Beit Avtinas for incense practice, full day of service, dusk purification

quizSeeds.question: How many chambers and gates did the Temple's inner courtyard (Azara) have?
quizSeeds.answer: Six chambers, three along the north wall and three along the south wall, and seven gates.

quizSeeds.question: The Mishna never states directly which side of the Azara the Parhedrin chamber was on. How does the Gemara establish that it was in the south?
quizSeeds.answer: By adjacency inference: the Mishna in Tamid places the KG's immersion bath next to the Parhedrin chamber, and the immersion bath is independently known to be in the south, near the Water Gate. Since the chamber sits next to the bath, it must also be in the south.

sugyot.finalRuling: Parhedrin chamber = south side of the Azara, adjacent to the immersion facility.

glossary.en: KG's personal separation chamber during the seven days before Yom Kippur, located on the south side of the inner courtyard.
Parvah Chamber - south side of the Azara, with a sanctified immersion pool on its roof used for five of the KG's Yom Kippur immersions.

display.title: Efficient placement, deliberate difficulty: south wins, but the walking stays
display.whats: The Gemara debates whether the Parhedrin chamber was on the north or south side of the Azara. The debate hinges on the KG's daily routine during the seven days: if Parhedrin is in the south, his morning routine is efficient (rise, relieve self, immerse at the nearby Water Gate, then walk north to Beit Avtinas). If in the north, his routine involves more walking. The Gemara concludes: south is more defensible. The difficulty of the all-day service imposed on the KG is also analyzed.
display.hint: The north-south debate about the Parhedrin chamber is really a debate about the KG's logistical convenience during his seven-day preparation. The Gemara's preferred answer puts Parhedrin in the south - closer to the Water Gate for morning immersion.

learning.learnerQuestion: Given two possible layouts, why does the Gemara settle on south for the Parhedrin chamber, and why would the Sages design the KG's routine to involve repeated back-and-forth walking at all?
learning.coreTension: A south placement makes the KG's morning efficient, but his daily routine still has him crossing the Azara repeatedly between immersion (south) and incense practice (north), which looks like avoidable inefficiency for someone the Temple should be accommodating.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara first settles the chamber's side by comparing the two possible routines: south placement keeps the morning sequence efficient, while north placement would force a redundant double crossing, so south is preferred as the more economical reading. It then reframes the remaining daily back-and-forth itself: the Sages did not minimize the KG's exertion because they wanted him to feel the weight of the preparation, preventing his elevated role from inflating his ego.
learning.resolution: Parhedrin is confirmed in the south based on routine efficiency, but the routine's residual difficulty is not a design flaw; it is intentional, meant to keep the KG humble despite his status.

takeaway.text: Not every apparent inefficiency in a religious procedure is an oversight to be optimized away; some friction is deliberately preserved because the difficulty itself serves a formative purpose.

learning.ahaMoment: The physical difficulty of the KG's preparation is deliberate: the Sages could have arranged an easier sequence, but they chose the harder path on purpose to keep the KG humble.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners who just resolved the north/south question by efficiency logic tend to assume the Gemara is optimizing the KG's routine throughout, then are surprised when it turns around and defends the remaining difficulty as intentional rather than something to streamline further.
learning.memoryAnchor: Parhedrin in the south. Daily routine: south for morning needs, north for incense practice. The leftover difficulty is intentional, to prevent pride.

argumentFlow.label: Where is the Parhedrin: north or south?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: We established the immersion bath was in the south (adjacent to Parhedrin). But is this Parhedrin chamber itself in the north or south? The logic from the immersion bath suggests south.

argumentFlow.label: If south: efficient morning routine
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If Parhedrin is in the south: the KG wakes up, relieves himself (needs immersion for entering the Azara), goes south to the Water Gate area, immerses, then walks north to Beit Avtinas for incense practice. This is an efficient path.

argumentFlow.label: If north: roundabout routine
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: If Parhedrin is in the north: the KG wakes up in the north, must go south to the Water Gate to immerse, then can return north to Beit Avtinas. This doubles his walking and is less efficient.

argumentFlow.label: Parhedrin is in the south - confirmed
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The south placement is more logical given the routine. Parhedrin in the south means the KG's immersion bath is right there, making his morning routine streamlined. This is the accepted conclusion.

argumentFlow.label: Why impose such difficulty on the KG?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Why does the seven-day preparation impose all this physical difficulty on the KG? The Gemara answers: to prevent his ego from becoming puffed up. The difficulty keeps him focused and humble.

concepts.def: Immersion chamber - the ritual bath used by the KG for his ten Yom Kippur immersions. Five immersions were done in the sanctified pool atop the Parvah chamber; the rest in a regular mikveh near the Water Gate.

requiresUnderstanding: The KG's daily routine during seven days preparation

misconceptions.misconception: The walking back and forth in the KG's daily routine is an accidental or unavoidable byproduct of the Temple's layout.
misconceptions.correction: The Gemara presents it as deliberately preserved: the Sages wanted the KG to experience some physical difficulty during his preparation so that his elevated status would not inflate his ego.

visualizableElements.desc: Two routing diagrams: (A) Parhedrin in south - efficient morning route; (B) Parhedrin in north - roundabout route. Shows why south is preferred.

quizSeeds.question: Why does the Gemara conclude that the Parhedrin chamber was in the south of the Azara rather than the north?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the KG's morning routine requires going south to the Water Gate area for immersion. If Parhedrin were in the north, he would walk south for immersion and then back north to Beit Avtinas, an inefficient double circuit that a south placement avoids.

quizSeeds.question: After concluding south is more efficient, why does the Gemara still defend the KG's remaining back-and-forth walking as necessary rather than streamlining it further?
quizSeeds.answer: Because the Sages deliberately preserved some physical difficulty in the KG's preparation to prevent his elevated status from inflating his ego, not because the routine could not be made easier.

sugyot.finalRuling: Parhedrin is in the south. The imposed difficulty is intentional and serves a mussar function: keeping the KG humble.

display.title: God's agent or Israel's agent? The question opens, the answer waits for 19b
display.whats: A brief but theologically significant debate: do the kohanim act as agents (shlichim) of God when offering sacrifices, or as agents of the Jewish people? This distinction has practical halakhic consequences - if they are God's agents, a kohen who is forbidden from benefiting from a particular person could still bring that person's sacrifice. If they are Israel's agents, such service would be forbidden.
display.hint: The agency question (shlichut) matters: if kohanim = God's agents, then the person offering has no legal 'hand' in the service (God is the effective party). If kohanim = Israel's agents, then the person's status (e.g., a vow against the kohen) affects whether the service is valid.

learning.learnerQuestion: When a kohen offers someone's sacrifice, is he legally acting on behalf of God, or on behalf of the person who brought the offering?
learning.coreTension: The classification is not just abstract theology: if a person has vowed not to benefit from a particular kohen, the answer determines whether that kohen may still bring his sacrifice, since the case turns on whose agent the kohen actually is.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara frames the question as a live dispute with practical stakes rather than settling it here: if kohanim are God's agents, the offerer has no direct legal 'hand' in the kohen's actions, so a forbidden-benefit vow would not block the service; if kohanim are Israel's agents, the personal relationship between kohen and offerer carries through and a forbidden-benefit vow could disqualify it.

takeaway.text: Some halakhic questions are introduced with their full practical stakes laid out before any resolution is reached, and the sugya's job at that stage is to make clear why the answer matters, not to supply it yet.

learning.ahaMoment: Agency law (shlichut) applied to Temple service: who is the 'real' party when a kohen brings a sacrifice? The answer determines whether personal legal relationships between the kohen and the offerer matter at all.
learning.learningBlocker: Learners often expect every question the Gemara raises to be answered within the same sugya, and may search this page for a resolution that the text deliberately withholds until 19b.
learning.memoryAnchor: Kohanim: shlichei shamayim (God's agents) vs shlichei Yisrael (Israel's agents). God's agents: a forbidden-benefit vow does not block the service. Israel's agents: it might. Answer on 19b.

argumentFlow.label: Are kohanim agents of God or agents of Israel?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The question arises: when a kohen offers a sacrifice, is he acting as God's agent (and the service proceeds regardless of the kohen's personal relationship with the offerer) or as Israel's agent (and personal legal relationships between kohen and offerer could invalidate the service)?

concepts.def: Agency law - the principle that an agent acts on behalf of the principal. 'An agent is like the principal himself.' The question is whether the kohen-offerer relationship is principal-agent (Israel's agent) or messenger-sender (God's agent).

requiresUnderstanding: Forbidden-benefit vows (nedarim) that could block kohen-offerer relationships

misconceptions.misconception: The question of whether kohanim are God's agents or Israel's agents is purely theological speculation with no practical consequence.
misconceptions.correction: It directly determines a practical case: whether a kohen bound by another person's forbidden-benefit vow may still bring that person's sacrifice.

visualizableElements.desc: Two diagrams: (A) shlichei shamayim - kohen -> God -> sacrifice, offerer has no direct connection; (B) shlichei Yisrael - offerer -> kohen (as agent) -> sacrifice, kohen-offerer relationship matters

quizSeeds.question: What is the practical halakhic difference between kohanim being 'God's agents' versus 'Israel's agents' when it comes to a forbidden-benefit vow against a specific kohen?
quizSeeds.answer: If kohanim are God's agents, the kohen can still bring that person's sacrifice because the offerer has no direct legal hand in the kohen's act. If kohanim are Israel's agents, the personal relationship carries through and the vow could block the kohen from performing that service.

quizSeeds.question: According to Rashi, what position does Rav Huna take on this question, and what is the practical upshot?
quizSeeds.answer: Rav Huna holds that kohanim are shelichei shamayim, agents of Heaven. The upshot is that even a kohen whom the offerer has vowed not to benefit from can still bring that person's sacrifice, since the vow does not reach an act done on God's behalf.

glossary.en: Agents of Heaven - one position on kohanim's status: they act for God, not for the individual offerer.
KG's personal separation chamber during the seven days before Yom Kippur, located on the south side of the inner courtyard.
Parvah Chamber - south side of the Azara, with a sanctified immersion pool on its roof used for five of the KG's Yom Kippur immersions.
Agents of Heaven - one position on kohanim's status: they act for God, not for the individual offerer.

rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: 'to teach him the incense-scooping' - they take the KG to Beit Avtinas to practice the hanifa (scooping) technique for the Yom Kippur incense service.
Rashi: 'Lishkat Parhedrin - to sleep there' - the KG's chamber for his seven-day stay.
Rashi: 'Lishkat Avtinas - to learn the incense-scooping' - the second KG chamber, for practicing the incense service.
Rashi: 'on its roof' - the Parvah chamber's roof had a water source fed by the Etam spring for immersion.
Rashi: 'musiba' - a spiral stone staircase, like a mill wheel winding around a central post.
Rashi: 'to ascend to the roof of Beit Parvah' - you go up the spiral staircase to reach the immersion pool on the roof.
Rashi: 'Lishkat HaGolah' - named after a cistern dug by the returning exiles (golah); the water for the Temple was stored there.
Rashi: 'behind both of them' - behind both Lishkat HaGolah and Lishkat HaGazit.
Rashi: 'and the roof of all three is level' - the same roof covers all three northern chambers.
Rashi: 'Sha'ar HaDalak' (Fuel Gate) / 'Sha'ar HaKorban' (Sacrifice Gate) - Rashi admits he does not know how these gates got their names.
Rashi: 'Sha'ar HaMayim (Water Gate)' - through this gate the water for Sukkot water-drawing (nisuch ha-mayim) was brought in, as mentioned in Shekalim.
Rashi: 'ten sanctifications' - the KG sanctifies (immerses and washes) his hands and feet ten times on Yom Kippur, corresponding to five changes of garments.
Rashi: 'on the same day' (Yom Kippur) - these ten immersions/washings all occur within Yom Kippur itself.
Rashi: 'and all of them in sanctity' - all ten immersions on Yom Kippur must be done in a sanctified location (the Parvah roof pool or other dedicated spot).
Rashi: 'except this one' - the first regular immersion (required of anyone entering the Azara) is not counted among the ten Yom Kippur immersions.
Rashi: 'not because of Yom Kippur but because of the Azara' - the regular immersion before entering the Azara is a year-round requirement, not Yom Kippur-specific.
Rashi: 'on the roof of Beit Parvah' - the sanctified immersion pool on the Parvah chamber's roof is where the five special Yom Kippur immersions occur.
Rashi: 'since it is built within the sanctified Azara' - the Parvah roof pool is sanctified because the building it is on stands within the Azara.
Rashi: 'except for this immersion' - only the first (pre-Azara) immersion is not in a sanctified location; all Yom Kippur immersions must be sanctified.
Rashi: 'and next to his chamber' - the immersion bath was adjacent to the KG's Parhedrin chamber - hence the Parhedrin chamber must be near the immersion facility.
Rashi: 'therefore the Parhedrin chamber is in the south' - because the immersion bath is in the south (near the Water Gate), and Parhedrin is adjacent to it.
Rashi: 'it is more probable that the Parhedrin chamber is in the south' - stronger evidence for south than north.
Rashi: 'why is he rising early?' - each morning during the seven days, when the KG wakes early, he needs to use the bathroom, requiring a general immersion to enter the Azara.
Rashi: 'and covers his feet' - a euphemism for relieving himself, like 'he covered his feet' in the book of Samuel about Saul in the cave.
Rashi: 'and immerses' - after relieving himself, the KG immerses at the Water Gate (south) before entering the Azara.
Rashi: 'and goes north to his chamber' - after the south immersion, he walks north to the Beit Avtinas chamber to practice the incense scooping.
Rashi: 'and immerses there according to R. Akiva' - the purification sprinkling in the evening (per R. Akiva's position) makes the KG tamei, requiring immersion.
Rashi: 'and rests' - after the evening immersion and waiting for nightfall, the KG rests in the Parhedrin chamber in the south.
Rashi: 'if Parhedrin were in the north, you would have given him extra work' - if north, the KG would need to walk south for immersion and back north twice every day.
Rashi: 'rises early and covers his feet' - rises from bed in the north (hypothetically) and goes south to the Water Gate for immersion.
Rashi: 'and goes north' - after the south immersion, walks north to Beit Avtinas for incense practice.
Rashi: 'and immerses and finishes the incense practice' - at Beit Avtinas (which is in the north, at least the northern section).
Rashi: 'goes to the Temple and does the service all day' - the seven-day service practice continues throughout the day.
Rashi: 'as we said at the beginning of the chapter' - referring back to the R. Akiva para aduma discussion from 14a-14b.
Rashi: 'goes south and immerses' - the evening purification sprinkling (from para aduma) makes the KG impure; he immerses at the Water Gate to restore purity.
Rashi: 'and rests' - after the evening immersion, goes back to rest in the Parhedrin chamber (south if accepted).
Rashi: 'why not? For it is like him having two chambers close together' - if Parhedrin were in the south and Avtinas in the north, would it not be better to put both in the same area?
Rashi: the agency question is introduced: when the KG or any kohen brings a sacrifice, are they God's agents or Israel's agents? This matters for forbidden-benefit scenarios.
Rashi: 'may it be said this is a refutation of Rav Huna?' - citing the mishna 'you are our agent' (the oath ceremony) as suggesting kohanim = Israel's agents.
Rashi: Rav Huna says kohanim are God's agents (shlichei shamayim) - hence even someone forbidden to benefit from the kohen can have his sacrifice offered.
Rashi: the practical case: someone took a vow forbidding benefit from a specific kohen - can that kohen still bring his sacrifice? Depends on agent-of-God vs agent-of-Israel.
Rashi: 'shelichei shamayim ninhu' - they are Heaven's agents. In Rav Huna's view, the vow is irrelevant to the sacrifice.
Rashi: 'the case: he is forbidden to benefit from him' - this is about nedarim (vows), not about priestly status.
Rashi: the case where the kohen made a vow against the offerer - can he still bring the sacrifice if he is God's agent (not personally acting for the offerer)?
Rashi: 'does not receive benefit, only sanctifies the sacrifice' - if the kohen is God's agent, he is not 'serving' the offerer personally and the vow may not apply.
Rashi: transition to the oath ceremony at Beit Avtinas - 'let it be said the mishna is a refutation of Rav Huna' from the phrase 'you are our agent.'
Rashi: the oath says 'attah shluchenu' (you are our agent) - does this phrase prove the kohanim are Israel's agents, contradicting Rav Huna?
